title: Running Multiple Sites Without Losing Your Mind
subtitle: One great cafe is hard. Two consistent cafes is a completely different discipline.

tools: T190-01 · New Site Opening Master Timeline

sections.heading: The Multi-Site Mindset Shift
sections.body:
Running one cafe is about craft. Running multiple cafes is about systems.

The skills that made you a great single-site operator — personal relationships with every customer, hands-on quality control, intuitive decision-making — do not scale. You cannot be in two places at once, and the cafe you are not in will drift.

The mindset shift:

| Single-Site Thinking | Multi-Site Thinking |
|---------------------|--------------------|
| I set the standard by being present | Standards are set by documentation and training |
| I know what is happening because I see it | I know what is happening because of reporting |
| Quality depends on me | Quality depends on systems and people |
| I make all decisions | Managers make daily decisions; I make strategic ones |
| I hire people to help me | I hire people to replace me |

This shift is uncomfortable. Letting go of daily control feels like letting go of quality. But the truth is the opposite: a system-dependent operation is more consistent than a person-dependent one, because systems do not have bad days, do not call in sick, and do not burn out.

The owner's role in a multi-site operation is architect, not bricklayer. Design the systems. Hire and develop the managers. Monitor the outcomes. Intervene only when the system fails.

sections.heading: Brand Consistency Across Sites
sections.body:
Use T187-01 (Brand Audit Scorecard) to measure consistency across sites.

A customer should not be able to tell which site they are in by the quality of the coffee, the warmth of the greeting, or the cleanliness of the space. They should be able to tell by the local character — different neighbourhood, different regulars, slightly different vibe — but the core experience should be the same.

**Mystery shopper programme:**
Hire friends, family, or a professional service to visit each site monthly and score:
- Coffee quality (shot, milk, temperature, presentation)
- Service warmth (greeting, eye contact, farewell)
- Cleanliness (tables, floor, toilet, counter)
- Speed (queue to coffee-in-hand)
- Food quality (freshness, presentation, taste)
- Atmosphere (music, lighting, temperature)

Score each element 1–10. Average across visits. Compare sites.

**Acceptable variance:** ±5% between sites on the same metric.
**Unacceptable variance:** >15% between sites on any metric — this indicates a systemic problem at one site.

Share mystery shopper results with site managers. Not as a punishment — as a diagnostic tool. "Site B is scoring 6/10 on greeting warmth while Site A scores 9/10. What is different? How do we close the gap?"

The brand audit should cover visual consistency too: signage, menu boards, cup branding, staff uniforms, social media. A customer who follows you on Instagram and visits a different site should recognise what they see.

sections.heading: The SOP Library
sections.body:
Every process that matters needs a Standard Operating Procedure. Use T188-01 (SOP Template) to build a library that is accessible, current, and used.

**Core SOPs every multi-site operation needs:**

*Coffee:*
- Grinder calibration procedure
- Espresso recipe and quality standards
- Milk steaming standards
- Filter/batch brew procedure
- Machine cleaning (daily, weekly, quarterly)

*Food:*
- Recipe cards for every menu item
- Food safety and handling procedures
- Display cabinet management
- Allergen management protocol

*Service:*
- Customer greeting and service standards
- Complaint handling procedure
- Till operation and payment processing
- Takeaway and delivery order handling

*Operations:*
- Opening checklist
- Closing checklist
- Shift handover procedure
- Cash handling and reconciliation
- Stock ordering and receiving

*People:*
- Onboarding programme
- Performance review process
- Disciplinary procedure
- Rota planning guidelines

**SOP format principles:**
- One page per procedure (maximum two)
- Step-by-step numbered instructions
- Photos where helpful
- Clear owner (who is responsible for this process)
- Version number and review date
- Accessible at every site (digital shared drive + printed at point of use)

**SOP compliance monitoring:**
Track compliance monthly. Are the SOPs being followed? How do you know? Through mystery shoppers, quality checks, financial data, and manager observation. An unmonitored SOP is a suggestion, not a standard.

sections.heading: Cross-Site Financial Benchmarking
sections.body:
Use T189-01 (Cross-Site Financial Dashboard) to compare sites weekly.

**Key metrics to compare:**

| Metric | Site A | Site B | Variance | Action |
|--------|--------|--------|----------|--------|
| Revenue (weekly) | | | | |
| ATV | | | | |
| Food attachment % | | | | |
| Labour cost % | | | | |
| Food cost % | | | | |
| Coffee cost % | | | | |
| Waste % | | | | |
| Revenue per labour hour | | | | |
| Transactions per hour (peak) | | | | |

**What variance tells you:**

- **Revenue variance >15%** (on comparable sites): Different customer volumes or ATV. Investigate marketing, location factors, or team performance.
- **Labour cost variance >5%**: One site is overstaffed relative to revenue. Review rota vs. demand alignment.
- **Food cost variance >3%**: Portion control, waste, or supplier pricing differs. Investigate.
- **ATV variance >10%**: One team is upselling better than the other. Share their approach.

The goal is not identical numbers — sites have different locations, footfall, and customer mixes. The goal is understanding *why* numbers differ and whether the difference is acceptable or actionable.

Review the dashboard in a weekly multi-site meeting. Each site manager presents their numbers and explains variances. This creates accountability and shared learning.

sections.heading: The Area Manager Role
sections.body:
At 3+ sites, the owner cannot effectively oversee every location. The area manager role becomes essential.

Use T191-01 (Area Manager Role Description):

**Core responsibilities:**
- Visit each site at least twice monthly (ideally weekly)
- Conduct brand consistency audits
- Review financial performance with site managers
- Ensure SOP compliance
- Support site managers with hiring, training, and performance issues
- Escalate significant problems to the owner
- Facilitate cross-site learning

**What the area manager is NOT:**
- A shift manager who covers multiple sites
- A spy who reports on staff
- The owner's substitute for being involved

**Visit structure (2-hour site visit):**
1. Walk the floor — observe service, cleanliness, atmosphere (15 min)
2. Check compliance — checklists, logs, temperatures, maintenance (15 min)
3. Sit with the site manager — review numbers, discuss issues, plan (30 min)
4. Observe service during peak or busy period (30 min)
5. Coach one team member on a specific skill (15 min)
6. Write visit report with action items (15 min)

**Who to hire:** Ideally someone who has managed a cafe themselves. They need to understand the reality of a morning rush, a broken machine, and a staff member who does not show up. Credibility on the floor matters more than management theory.

The area manager is the owner's eyes, ears, and hands across the estate. Invest in this role properly — it is the single most important hire in a multi-site operation.

sections.heading: Central Procurement and Shared Learning
sections.body:
Multiple sites create buying power. Use T193-01 (Procurement Audit Template) to leverage it.

**Central procurement benefits:**
- **Volume discounts:** Ordering coffee, milk, and packaging for 3 sites instead of 1 gives negotiating leverage. Even a 5% discount on your biggest cost line (coffee beans) delivers meaningful savings.
- **Consistency:** All sites use the same beans, the same milk, the same cups. The customer experience is identical.
- **Simplified supplier management:** One relationship, one delivery schedule, one invoice instead of three.

**What to centralise:**
- Coffee beans (must be identical across sites)
- Milk and alternative milks (same brands, same products)
- Packaging (cups, lids, bags — branded if possible)
- Cleaning products
- Key food suppliers (bakery, dairy)

**What to keep local:**
- Fresh produce (local sourcing adds character)
- Emergency top-ups (the local cash-and-carry when you run out)
- Site-specific items (local art, unique furniture)

**Cross-site learning:**
Use T194-01 (Best Practice Submission Form) to create a formal way to share what works.

When Site A discovers that rearranging the counter reduces queue time by 30 seconds, that learning should reach Site B within a week. When Site B develops a new seasonal drink that outsells everything, Site A should have the recipe card by the following Monday.

Mechanisms:
- Monthly multi-site manager meeting (in person or video)
- Shared digital workspace (Notion, Google Drive, WhatsApp group)
- Quarterly cross-site staff swap (a barista from Site A works a shift at Site B)
- Annual all-hands meeting (every team member from every site, together)

The greatest competitive advantage of a multi-site operation is the ability to learn faster than a single site. Every location is an experiment. Harvest the learning and distribute it — that is how small chains outperform large ones.
